Bad prompt: "How many dogs are in this image?"
→ Result: Incorrect count (hallucination)

Good prompt: "I'll visually scan the image from left to right, 
focusing on each individual dog. Starting on the left, I see..."
→ Result: Correct count (9 dogs)

- Forces systematic scanning
- Reduces creative guessing
- Makes the reasoning explicit

"You are a UI designer comparing these two versions.
Identify layout changes between Image 1 and Image 2."

- Defines expected expertise level
- Reduces hallucination on ambiguous comparisons
- Sets context for analysis

Example 1:
[Image of speedometer showing 50 km/h]
Answer: "50 km/h"

Example 2:
[Image of speedometer showing 120 km/h]
Answer: "120 km/h"

Now answer:
[Image of speedometer showing ? km/h]
Answer: "?"
Few-shot with images doesn't always work, but worth trying.

- Enforces consistent format
- Reduces hallucination through constraints
- Makes results programmable
Claude processes content sequentially. Placing all images first gives complete visual context before encountering text.

Bad: "What do you see?"
Good: "Extract the restaurant name, total amount, and date from this receipt"
Quick Checklist for Optimal Image Quality
Capture at higher resolution, ensure sharp focus
Be specific: "Extract" not "What do you see?"
Move all images to top of prompt
Increase contrast or use PNG format
Understanding the internals of image quality tips helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with image quality tips has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying image quality tips in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Claude's vision system processes images at their original resolution up to a maximum of 8000×8000 pixels, with individual image size capped at 5MB (20MB via base64 in some configurations). The practical minimum resolution for useful image analysis depends on the content: for charts and diagrams, 800×600 is workable but 1200×900 or higher is recommended for accurate data point reading. For document OCR (reading text from images), the text must be legible at the rendering size — aim for at least 150 DPI equivalent, with 300 DPI preferred for small text. For photographs, 512×512 is the practical minimum for object recognition, though 1024×768 or higher gives Claude more signal for fine detail. Images that are too large add unnecessary latency and cost without improving quality — Claude will internally downscale very large images before processing, so sending a 6000×4000 photo for simple object detection is wasteful.
Charts and graphs: minimum 800×600, recommended 1200×900 to resolve individual data points and labels
OCR / text extraction: target 150 DPI minimum, 300 DPI preferred for small fonts under 10pt
UI screenshots: 1x resolution is sufficient; 2x/Retina screenshots are fine but not necessary
Photographs: 512×512 minimum for basic recognition, 1024×768+ for fine detail analysis
Technical diagrams: 1200×800 minimum to resolve labels, arrows, and small connector lines
Maximum size: 5MB per image; above this, preprocess to reduce size before sending
Cost consideration: image tokens scale with resolution — cap screenshots at 1920×1080 to avoid overspending

The image format you choose affects file size, quality preservation, and Claude's ability to read specific content types. JPEG is ideal for photographs and complex imagery where minor compression artifacts are acceptable — it achieves 5-10x size reduction versus PNG with minimal visible quality loss at quality settings of 75-85. PNG is better for screenshots, diagrams, charts, and anything containing text or sharp edges: its lossless compression preserves pixel-perfect clarity that JPEG compression destroys, particularly for sub-pixel rendering of small fonts. WebP combines the advantages of both — lossless WebP matches PNG quality at roughly 25% smaller size, while lossy WebP outperforms JPEG at equivalent quality settings. GIF is generally not recommended for Claude: it's limited to 256 colors, which reduces the color information available for chart analysis and causes banding in photographic content. When in doubt, use PNG for anything containing text or sharp geometric shapes, and JPEG or WebP for photographs.
PNG: use for screenshots, diagrams, charts, and any image containing text — lossless = no artifacts
JPEG (quality 75-85): use for photographs where compression artifacts won't impede analysis
WebP lossless: best overall choice where format support exists — PNG quality at 25% smaller file size
WebP lossy: better than JPEG at equivalent quality settings; good for photographic content
GIF: avoid — 256-color limit degrades chart analysis and introduces banding in photos
BMP/TIFF: uncompressed; same quality as PNG but much larger file sizes with no benefit
SVG: Claude cannot process SVG directly; rasterize to PNG before sending diagrams in SVG format

Preprocessing images before sending them to Claude's API can significantly improve both quality and cost efficiency. The key transformations are: resize to the useful resolution (no larger than needed), convert to the optimal format, enhance contrast for document images, and crop to the region of interest. Cropping is particularly underused: if you're asking Claude to analyze a specific chart in a slide, crop out the slide chrome and surrounding whitespace before sending. This reduces token count and forces Claude's attention onto the relevant content. For OCR tasks, preprocessing with contrast enhancement and sharpening using Pillow or OpenCV can meaningfully improve accuracy on low-quality scans. Always preprocess in a pipeline so your API caller sends optimized images automatically rather than requiring manual preparation.

from PIL import Image, ImageEnhance, ImageFilter
import io
import base64
import anthropic

def preprocess_image(
    image_path: str,
    max_width: int = 1920,
    max_height: int = 1080,
    quality: int = 85,
    enhance_for_ocr: bool = False,
) -> str:
    """
    Preprocess an image for Claude API:
    - Resize to max dimensions (preserving aspect ratio)
    - Optionally enhance contrast/sharpness for OCR
    - Convert to JPEG bytes
    - Return as base64-encoded data URI
    """
    with Image.open(image_path) as img:
        # Convert RGBA/P to RGB (JPEG doesn't support alpha)
        if img.mode in ("RGBA", "P", "LA"):
            background = Image.new("RGB", img.size, (255, 255, 255))
            if img.mode == "P":
                img = img.convert("RGBA")
            background.paste(img, mask=img.split()[-1] if img.mode in ("RGBA", "LA") else None)
            img = background
        elif img.mode != "RGB":
            img = img.convert("RGB")

        # Resize if larger than max dimensions
        img.thumbnail((max_width, max_height), Image.LANCZOS)

        # Optional: enhance for OCR (document scanning)
        if enhance_for_ocr:
            img = ImageEnhance.Contrast(img).enhance(1.5)   # Boost contrast
            img = ImageEnhance.Sharpness(img).enhance(2.0)  # Sharpen edges
            img = img.filter(ImageFilter.UnsharpMask(radius=1, percent=50))

        # Encode to JPEG bytes
        buffer = io.BytesIO()
        img.save(buffer, format="JPEG", quality=quality, optimize=True)
        buffer.seek(0)

        b64 = base64.standard_b64encode(buffer.read()).decode("utf-8")
        return f"data:image/jpeg;base64,{b64}"

def analyze_image(image_path: str, question: str, ocr_mode: bool = False) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    image_uri = preprocess_image(image_path, enhance_for_ocr=ocr_mode)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": [
                {
                    "type": "image",
                    "source": {"type": "base64", "media_type": "image/jpeg", "data": image_uri.split(",")[1]}
                },
                {"type": "text", "text": question}
            ]
        }]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Analyze a chart
result = analyze_image("sales_chart.png", "What was the highest revenue month and its value?")
print(result)

# OCR a scanned document with enhancement
text = analyze_image("scanned_doc.jpg", "Extract all text from this document.", ocr_mode=True)
print(text)

Claude's API accepts multiple images in a single message — you include multiple content blocks of type 'image' in the user message array. The ordering of images in the request matters: Claude processes the content array in sequence, so if your prompt refers to 'the first chart' and 'the second chart', those must correspond to the first and second image blocks respectively. When comparing images, a well-structured request includes all images first, followed by the text question that references them — this ensures Claude has full visual context before reading the analytical question. There is a practical limit of about 20 images per request before context becomes unwieldy and quality degrades; for large image sets, batch into groups and aggregate responses rather than sending all at once.
Order matters: image blocks are indexed in sequence; use 'first image', 'second image' to reference them explicitly
Structure: include all images first in the content array, then the text question that references them
Practical limit: 10-20 images per request is workable; beyond that, quality and coherence degrade
For comparison tasks: explicitly label images in your prompt ('the left/right image', 'Chart A/B') to avoid ambiguity
Token cost: each image costs tokens proportional to its pixel count — scale down all images when sending multiples
Batch large sets: process 10 images at a time and aggregate results rather than sending 50+ in one call
Annotate images: adding numbered labels in the image itself (e.g., overlaid text '1', '2') helps Claude track which is which
Optimizing Images for OCR Use Cases

When the primary goal is extracting text from an image — scanned documents, receipts, screenshots of terminal output, photos of whiteboards — several preprocessing steps dramatically improve Claude's accuracy. The most impactful is contrast normalization: increase contrast so text pixels are near black and background pixels are near white. Next is deskewing: if the camera angle or scanner bed caused the document to appear tilted, rotate it to horizontal/vertical alignment. For documents with complex backgrounds (watermarks, colored paper), apply background removal or binarization to convert to pure black-and-white. Finally, for handwritten content, explicit prompting ('this is handwritten text, transcribe it including corrections for likely OCR errors') tends to outperform silent extraction. Always validate OCR outputs against expected patterns — phone numbers should match phone number formats, dates should parse successfully — to catch the occasional hallucinated character.
Contrast normalization: threshold to near-black text on near-white background reduces ambiguous pixels
Deskewing: rotate tilted scans to horizontal alignment; even 3-5 degree tilt degrades accuracy
Noise reduction: blur followed by threshold removes scanner grain and JPEG artifacts around text
Background removal: mask out complex backgrounds (watermarks, colored paper) before extraction
Resolution: 300 DPI is the standard target for OCR; below 150 DPI, small fonts become unreliable
Font size: text below 8pt in the original document is frequently misread even at 300 DPI
Prompt strategy: explicitly state 'this is a scanned document' and ask for structured output (key: value) where applicable
